goal: Generate, rig, clean up, and ship anime RPG humanoid source models as optimized web GLB or VRM runtime assets.

practicalUse: Vite preview, TypeScript build, GLB audit, and lightweight asset registration
Blender headless generation/export through the known remote Blender paths or BLENDER_BIN
Not suitable for local Hunyuan3D, TRELLIS, CharacterGen, StdGEN, UniRig, or SkinTokens inference because those routes expect NVIDIA/CUDA

tools.name: StdGEN
strengths: character-specific route instead of generic object reconstruction
semantic decomposition is a better fit for anime hair, face, body, and outfit separation
risks: research output still needs Blender cleanup before game runtime use
does not replace final topology, facial morphs, secondary motion, and animation QA
tools.projectUse: First GPU-backed experiment for Lyra and Mira when a CUDA machine is available.

tools.name: CharacterGen
strengths: designed around character generation
closer to anime humanoid use cases than generic image-to-object reconstruction
risks: game-ready rig, expression morphs, and secondary motion are not guaranteed
face likeness and hair topology must be reviewed and repaired
tools.projectUse: Second GPU-backed experiment for portrait-to-character candidate generation.

tools.name: Hunyuan3D 2.x
strengths: strong actively maintained general image-to-3D route
useful for fast silhouette, costume, and texture exploration
risks: not a game-character rigging solution
single generated mesh can merge hair, face, and clothing in ways that are hard to animate
tools.projectUse: Fallback generator for player, Lyra, and Mira when character-specific routes fail or are unavailable.

tools.name: TRELLIS
strengths: strong general 3D generation baseline
useful for comparing whether a generic model preserves costume silhouette better than character-specific research code
risks: not specialized for humanoid deformation
anime facial identity can drift

tools.name: InstantMesh
strengths: good baseline for turning a character image into an inspectable mesh
works as a comparison point before heavier character-specific methods
risks: does not produce production topology, rigging, expressions, or runtime materials
hair and clothing can become fused
tools.projectUse: Use as a visual candidate baseline when StdGEN or CharacterGen setup is too heavy.

tools.name: Wonder3D
strengths: useful for quickly producing multi-view geometry from one portrait
can expose whether the source portrait contains enough spatial information
risks: not anime-character-specific
mesh remains a cleanup candidate, not a final game asset
tools.projectUse: Use only as a fast reconstruction baseline.

tools.name: TripoSR
strengths: lighter than full diffusion 3D stacks
useful as a low-cost silhouette probe
risks: single-view reconstruction can flatten anime hair and clothing
does not produce game-ready topology, rig, expressions, or secondary motion
tools.projectUse: Use only for quick silhouette experiments when no GPU-backed route is available.

tools.name: SkinTokens
strengths: targets automatic skinning of 3D characters
fits after mesh generation and before Blender deformation cleanup
risks: auto skinning still needs walk, talk, hand, hair, and skirt deformation QA
VRM humanoid metadata, expressions, and spring bones are still separate authoring tasks
tools.projectUse: Primary auto-skinning experiment after a candidate mesh has acceptable silhouette.

tools.name: UniRig
strengths: targets automatic skeleton and skinning for generated characters
fits the stage between mesh generation and Blender cleanup
risks: auto-rig output still needs deformation review
facial morphs and secondary-motion bones still need manual authoring
tools.projectUse: Fallback auto-rig pass when SkinTokens is unsuitable.

tools.name: VRM Addon for Blender
expectedOutput: VRM avatar with humanoid metadata, expressions, and spring bones
strengths: matches anime avatar workflow
supports the VRM route for expressions, MToon-like materials, lookAt, and spring bones
risks: requires correct humanoid setup
export validation must be enforced before runtime promotion
tools.projectUse: Use for Lyra and major NPCs if GLB is not expressive enough.

tools.name: @pixiv/three-vrm
expectedOutput: Three.js VRM scene with expressions and humanoid helpers
strengths: native runtime bridge for VRM in Three.js
already present in package dependencies
risks: runtime cost must be controlled with lazy loading, LOD, and texture budgets
VRM assets still need animation and expression integration per character
tools.projectUse: Runtime path for expressive anime characters.

tools.name: MakeHuman
strengths: open source human body generation
useful when AI 3D generation is unavailable or inconsistent
risks: not anime-stylized by default
face, hair, clothing, textures, and expressions still require Blender authoring
tools.projectUse: Legal body-base fallback for player and supporting NPCs before anime stylization.

tools.name: MB-Lab
strengths: runs inside Blender
fits scripted source-model workflows better than GUI-only avatar tools
risks: not a final anime character generator
requires custom anime head, hair, clothing, textures, and expressions
tools.projectUse: Base-mesh fallback if all AI candidate routes are unavailable.

decision.firstExperiment: Run StdGEN or CharacterGen on a CUDA GPU machine for Lyra and Mira candidate meshes.
decision.fallbackExperiment: Run Hunyuan3D or TRELLIS for shape and texture candidates, then register outputs into this repository.
decision.riggingExperiment: Run SkinTokens first, UniRig second, then manually repair deformation in Blender.
decision.currentMiemiePath: Do not install heavy AI inference stacks on miemie unless an NVIDIA/CUDA GPU is added. Use the known remote Blender paths for deterministic headless source export and preview.
decision.finalAssetPath: Clean Blender source model with hand-checked topology, humanoid rig, skin weights, facial morphs, secondary-motion bones, and GLB or VRM export.
